sentences.text: I registered.
And I was supposed to get it back when I leave Yugoslavia for Italy.
I said I'm going to Italy, to Yugoslavia only.
But I left the train in Belgrade without documents and went to the best hotel in town.
The people are always more hesitant to arrest somebody who lives in a good hotel.
And registered there-- in Europe, when you registered, you had to give your passport and make all your notes, and then later the same evening, he wasn't back.
And when he asked for my passport, like I said, it's just the police because I am trying to get a prolongation, an extension of my visa.
And I stayed there and made several phone calls.
I couldn't do anything from there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One moment.
I then went to Zagreb again.
I do not know why.
I know it was in Belgrade.
It was in Belgrade.
And I met there a former girlfriend of mine, who many years ago, had been my girlfriend.
I met her.
And she was Serbian.
She had contact with the authorities.
She didn't have contact, but she could establish contact.
She was a very smart girl.
And I told her what I'm doing.
[INAUDIBLE] of each other for many years.
And the next day, I went-- the police came in the early morning and arrested me.
Because they found out that the passport and visa went from one of the hotel of the-- I assume though that [INAUDIBLE] found out and arrested me.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yes.
I had been-- I know why I went to Belgrade.
I wanted to-- the German embassy in Belgrade, to intervene with the [NON-ENGLISH] the German Danube company.
That they should give us a few more days, because if they go back, there will be a big scandal about it.
And the Germans wanted to [INAUDIBLE] quietly.
So-- but the German embassy wasn't ready to do anything.
And when they arrested me now, I gave this girl to hold the telegram [INAUDIBLE] the police.
The police were nice.
They permitted me to send the telegram.
I sent the telegram to the German embassy, to the [NON-ENGLISH] shipping company in Vienna.
[INAUDIBLE] excellent.
Strongly-- no.
Extension of stay for at least three or five days is recommended, or is necessary.
And this was on the evening of the 24th, on the afternoon of the 24th.
On the 24th of December.
There was no-- I knew that there would be nobody there.
And on the 25th, there would be nobody there.
Anyhow, in the meanwhile, I sent a telegram to the people in [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Sulina, said arrangements have been made to prolong trip-- order from Vienna, and Vienna consulate as follows.
Signed, so and so name, consulate in-- or embassy in Belgrade.
Somebody whom I knew [INAUDIBLE].
And the next morning, I took off, of course, as fast as I could, and I had no trouble getting into Italy.
And from Italy into Switzerland back again.
And then I was back again in Switzerland.
And this was the day-- this one day saved the I think.
Maybe there was something else in play, because there were very capable people working on meeting in Sulina too.
Yeah, I know, but the Turk wanted a guarantee that she will not be confiscated.
And Eri Jabotinski, the son of Jabotinski, said, I'm going with the ship.
And if they would capture the ship, I would be the first one whom they would arrest, and they would shoot me.
And he went with the ship.
And now you know the story which happens on the ship, with the ship.
The ship, the ship-- first of all, we took many more people than we were allowed to.
They had beds for supposedly with flour, and first they loaded some flour, some supplies for the people for the trip.
And then they were filled with people.
And we took many more people.
I don't remember the figure.
But 2,120 or so ended up on the And the went-- had the shortest trip of any of the ships.
It went through the Marmara [INAUDIBLE] into the Sea of Marmara and sailed.
But they got spotted by a British warship.
The British Navy had given orders to-- what inform [INAUDIBLE] ship was unpermitted refugees and arrest it and bring it to the next, the nearest port.
And so their landing party came on board, expecting all kinds of trouble.
We were about five or six men.
And there were 2,000 people on board.
And they said, I'm sorry, we have to confiscate you, and we have to direct you to the nearest British port.
[INAUDIBLE] Haifa.
So the captain was a very smart guy.
The Turks are very smart.
And he was not a Turk.
He was a Russian, I believe.
A White Russian.
He said, I don't go to Haifa.
I have orders to go to Alexandretta, which was in Syria.
So Alexandretta, I don't go to Haifa.
If you want me to go to Haifa, you have to give it to me in writing.
They got in writing that he's ordered by the British navy to go to Haifa.
That's what he wanted.
And when somebody started singing the 'God Save the King.' And they were stunned, the soldiers.
About the 'God Save the King.' These are their enemies.
And they looked at them as contraband.
They got the order to confiscate the contraband.
That's what the order which from the Navy had come.
And they were brought to Haifa.
And the ship couldn't be confiscated.
The Turks [INAUDIBLE] because it's bent against their will.
There was another instance where the ship's captain was very smart.
That was the Noemi Julia.
The ship was called Noemi Julia.
It was a revisionist ship.
It was not my ship.
It was by [INAUDIBLE] organized the ship.
But we worked very closely together.
And on the 1st of September, the day I was in Athens, it was passing through the Marmara, the Sea of Marmara.
And he sent a telegram to the British consulate [INAUDIBLE] Izmir, of Izmir, saying I have 800 people on board who all want to volunteer for the British forces.
What can I do with them?
Can I bring them to you?
Or, I intend to bring them to you.
Is this OK?

And he got the answer, if they're in need of anything, you know, or they have [INAUDIBLE] going on a day the war was declared so the consulate, all the citizens who lived outside were coming, wanting to get their passports in order and information and so on.
When if there was something he didn't need there, was 800 Jewish refugees.
So he gave-- said we can't take them.
And he actually told them, bring them to Haifa.
It was the nearest port.
But this was only writings, I believe.
But anyhow, with the permission of the British consulate in Izmir, the Noemi Julia went to Haifa.
The ship owners built [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bunks, wooden bunks.
They looked like the bunks in the concentration camps, which we did not know how they looked then.
But afterwards, I saw.
They were about, I think, 60 centimeters wide and about 80 centimeters high.
They're two-- three [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] levels.
And this is where they slept.
[INAUDIBLE] many of the ships, they didn't have enough bunks, so they couldn't sleep all at the same time.
They had to change.
Sometimes, and then others came and slept on this, and so on.
The conditions were very bad.
Well, very bad.
Food was usually plenty at the time of departure.
From Vienna, there was food for a week or so.
Only [INAUDIBLE] as the ship [INAUDIBLE] landed in between in various places on the Danube.
When you use [INAUDIBLE] refugees from Hitler coming, and they brought food and blankets.
We had-- they had organized this before.
I didn't organize it.
This is the organizers in Vienna, [INAUDIBLE].
And-- but the ships were very overcrowded.
And when the trip lasted longer, there was starvation, and there was thirst on the ship.
There was trouble, too.
There were very many unhappy people.
Every day was order of the day [INAUDIBLE] and on the even, the people there were dissatisfied.
That is, where the trip was similar and very crowded.
But it was only a few days, but they didn't know [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] order, and this was on the already, I think.
It was organized by the Betar in military form.
And the order of the day was such every morning.
And Eri Jabotinsky had only one order then.
And I don't know the words, but what it says in the Bible.
And the people were revolting.
And Moses told them, you want-- you want to go back to the fresh ports, fresh ports of Egypt?
Do you want to go back to-- he didn't say this.
This just was the order-- Moses [INAUDIBLE] said, and Moses said that you want to go back to the fresh ports of Egypt.
This was the order of the day.
Then there was an individual way of handling the revolutionaries.
There was one man who on the made very bad trouble.
Every day he was shouting and complaining about the conditions.
The slave drivers, slave dealers, and so on.
So what do you do with him?
There was a possibility of binding him to a post there and leaving him there for a few hours.
Whether this was ever done, I do not know, but he was threatened.
It was threatened to them all that we'd kick them out and leave them without papers in this country.
But somebody on this ship had a much better idea.
This guy had false teeth.
And they took his teeth away while he was asleep.
He couldn't speak anymore.
He was incapable of doing anything.
I do not know.
I do not remember, I would say.
I probably knew.
Yes.
They were given instructions for landing procedures.
They had to leave everything behind.
They had very little anyhow.
But everything had to be left behind.
And they had to appear as inconspicuous as possible.
One guy whom I interviewed later in Tel Aviv told me how inconspicuous he was.
He was just dressing when he heard we are leaving.
And he left with one shoe, only.
And half his face was shaven.
The other half was not shaven.
Inconspicuous.
But the Jews in Israel, which was then called Palestine, because [INAUDIBLE] Israel, were very helpful.
Once, when people were picked up by the police, they [INAUDIBLE] and freed them by force.
There was one case where a pocket thief-- a pickpocket, was caught by the police.
And he said, they are arresting me because I'm an illegal immigrant.
And the people freed him.
Somebody, yes.
He-- he went-- somebody with a bicycle took him to his home.
And he said, the best thing which impressed him most was that he could sit on a chair there.
I know that he walked around, walked in Tel Aviv, and some bicyclist came and told him.
But as a rule, the people went to a movie in Netanya.
And there they were hidden until the [INAUDIBLE] came to pick them up the next day.
And the [INAUDIBLE] brought them to the various [INAUDIBLE] places.
Yeah.
They couldn't land without them.
Because these ships couldn't go all the way to the-- go near to the beach, to the coast, from where you could swim.
Some could have swam, but most couldn't.
They had small ships from the land.
The Greeks got together with our people, with the [INAUDIBLE] people there.
And there were signs even when our ship approached.
And they were there.
But this very often didn't work.
There was no ship there to pick them up.
So they left and came back two days later again.
Yes.
Yeah.
Next.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I went-- I was in Switzerland when it arrived.
I went again to Athens.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I thought, somebody told me you can go-- you can fly.
I flew to Athens.
I remember now.
I flew to Athens, so that I didn't have to go through Romania.
And tried in Athens to get more work.
But it wasn't possible.
I made what was a very stupid mistake.
I saw in a cafe there, on the big square in Athens, in front of the [INAUDIBLE] the hotel, Grand Britain.
The big square with cafes [INAUDIBLE].
And then one of them there, as I passed, I saw an official of the British embassy sitting.
And he told me-- this must have been a short time after the arrived.
They did not know yet-- at least not [INAUDIBLE] which I had committed.
He told me, well we-- I said, hello.
He said, hello.
Well, we got your ship, didn't we?
Your I said yes, next time it will be much simpler.
We'll inform you when we leave.
And you will accompany us all the way, not only from the port.
And this was-- this teasing him didn't work out.
They made another intervention with the Athens government, and I was arrested and deported.
This was-- first of all, I stayed in prison [INAUDIBLE] for about three months.
I was arrested in about January 1940.
Or February 1940.
And was in prison, a real hole, I was there.
In the city prison.
And from this hole I was then brought to-- yeah.
From this hole they took me and wanted to return me.
I had a Portuguese visa amongst the visas which I had.
And they wanted to bring me to Portugal.
And they took me out, dressed me nicely so that the ship should take me.
And I walked handcuffed with them to the railroad station, and we took the train to Corinth.
Which is in the [INAUDIBLE].
I think it was Corinth.
A city there on the canal.
And there was a ship which was going to Britain.
And they wanted to put me on this ship to Britain.
That's what they wanted to do.
And I told them-- I told them that you will have trouble with me.
The British won't take me, nobody will take me.
And at the time, the shipping world was afraid of the Flying Dutchman.
They called him the Flying Dutchman.
You know, the Dutchman was the [INAUDIBLE] people who were on board and could nowhere land.
The people had-- there are ships which had Jews on board, and with the expectation of being able to set them off on some shore.
And they couldn't.
So they traveled with them all the time.
And I told them, if you want to become a Flying Dutchman, you take me.
I like to cruise the seas.
So they, of course-- she took me out again.
They didn't take me.
They left me to the Greeks.
And I went back again to the jail in [INAUDIBLE], which was a port-- it's the port of Athens, [INAUDIBLE].
That was before this, in Budapest.
I had one in Budapest.
Yeah.
That was when I was a working for the to get the out.
There were various organizations.
Many groups were in Czechoslovakia, in Vienna, and in Warsaw.
And all of them had this trouble with the consulates.
So I decided to, instead of paying all of them, I'd make myself consul of Liberia.
And Dr.
Kalman went with me to-- you cannot do anything phony in a fascist country like Hungary, where the police is very powerful.
And Kalman went with me to the police.
We made an appointment with the chief of police, which was granted because we said we would help him to get rid of the people who had come illegally into to Hungary, and whom he had in jails.
He had many dozens who had come illegally from Poland, and from Czechoslovakia, into Hungary.
Fled into Hungary.
And in Hungary they had no means to support, and no papers.
They brought them to jail.
And Hungary didn't like to feed them.
The same was true in Romania and in Bulgaria.
All the other countries wanted to get rid of the Jews.
Particularly, of the refugees who had no money.
Which we used in order to-- we used by promising him that if he let our ships past, that we will take a certain number of Jews from the prison.
And Kalman got his appointment because he said, here's a plan to get rid of all those Jews.
We made it as  far as the anteroom of the chief of police.
We never saw him.
But his executive officer came back, said what is it, and I told him the story.
That I want to make myself consul of Liberia.
And it will take months before this is found out.
And in the meanwhile, [INAUDIBLE] many ships will go, and on every ship we will take 50 or 70, I don't remember the number of Jews anymore, which we will take from his prisons.
So he went into the chief of police, and I don't know anymore, how long he's gone.
It didn't seem to me a long time.
At least I don't remember it as a long time.
He came out and said, that Mr.
So-and-so does not know anything, does not want to know anything, but you told him.
Well, so I went out, and I made myself consul of Liberia.
I bought stamps, consulate of Liberia.
And I had even a sign on an office, which said consulate of Liberia.

And in order to make the British believe that this is genuine, so that they shouldn't go right away and say to the police that this is a forgery, I informed one [INAUDIBLE] and he was-- I forgot his name.
I think his name was [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE].
And used the Hungarian name who wanted [INAUDIBLE] who was hanging around us to get information to bring it to the British.
And he would give information from the British to us.
A very small guy.
And I told him, you know this is a very big thing.
[INAUDIBLE] we can't tell you.
It is a secret.
But we tell you, it's a very big secret.
We will get visas, as many as we want to.
At the same time, I sent somebody to Belgrade.
He didn't have to telephone this.
That somebody should tell Belgrade-- let the British embassy in Belgrade know the same thing.
[INAUDIBLE] let them know, confidentially, that we have the way now, to get visas en masse.

Because the message that we gave these two informers was that we are negotiating with a small Latin American country-- it was not Latin America-- but we said, with a small Latin American country, to appoint me as consul.
So the information came, and the British didn't act there, because this was not the idea of forgery.
Your forgeries are-- you'd be doing this in the basement, in the darkness.
And so I had-- I told it to everybody that I'm now consul.
And we had the Jewish community of Budapest, called to meet in a banquet, and I was feted there.
And the waiters called me your excellency, and the Jews called me Herr Consul.
It was very funny.
At the same time, I informed-- at the same time, I informed our people of the same method.
You can do the same thing in Paris and in Warsaw and everywhere.
Make yourself consul, not of Liberia, of some other country.
Yes.
Sure they did.
Yeah.
